rulings.Case Title: JUNE E CLAUSEN VS. PACIFIC CRUISE SHIP TERMINALS LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Otis Elevators Compliance With Deposition Subpoena For Business Records; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Otis Elevator Company In The Sum Of 2,750 Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, April 29, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFFs JUNE CLAUSEN and LARRY CLAUSEN's Motion To Compel Otis Elevators Compliance With Deposition Subpoena For Business Records; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Otis Elevator Company In The Sum Of 2,750 Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear, hearing required. Plaintiff should be prepared to address its assertions that additional responsive documents exist within third party Otis' possession beyond what has been produced. Third party Otis should be prepared to address why it has not provided the table of contents for the identified manual or any other documents in response to Request Nos. 8 or 11. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MOATAZ MAASRANI VS. KHRG SUTTER UNION, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Approval Of Paga Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, April 29, 2019, Line 11, PLAINTIFF MOATAZ MAASRANI's Motion For Approval Of Paga Settlement. Plaintiff Moataz Maasrani's motion for approval of PAGA settlement is denied in part without prejudice. The Court finds that the overall amount of the proposed settlement is fair and adequate in view of the purposes and policies of PAGA, taking into account such factors as the adequacy of the civil penalties in light of the potential maximum if the matter were to go to verdict and the risks of litigation. (Lab. Code § 2699(l)(2); s.See Flores v. Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, Inc. (C.D. Cal. 2017) 253 F.Supp.3d 1074, 1077; O'Connor v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2016) 201 F.Supp.3d 1110, 1132-1135.) However, Plaintiff has failed to carry his burden to show that the amount of attorney's fees and costs sought is reasonable. (Lab. Code § 2699(g)(1).) Accordingly, the motion is denied without prejudice to Plaintiff's counsel filing sufficient additional information to carry that burden. Plaintiff seeks an award of $17,500 in fees, one-third of the gross settlement amount. Although the standard for an award of attorneys' fees under PAGA has not been clearly established, the usual method for calculating an attorneys' fees award under a statutory fee-shifting provision is the lodestar approach. (Laffitte v. Robert Half Int'l Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.5th 480, 500.) The Court acknowledges that in the analogous context of class action litigation, an attorneys' fees award is not per se unreasonable merely because it is calculated as a percentage of a common fund preserved or recovered by means of litigation; however, "[t]he choice of a fee calculation method is generally one within the discretion of the trial court, the goal under either the percentage or lodestar approach being the award of a reasonable fee to compensate counsel for their efforts." (Id. at 504.) Further, even if a court selects the percentage fee, it generally will double-check the reasonableness of the percentage fee through a lodestar calculation, which "provides a mechanism for bringing an objective measure of the work performed into the calculation of a reasonable attorney fee." (Id. at 488, 504.) Here, the Court intends to utilize both methods. Because Plaintiff has provided only very limited information regarding the tasks performed by counsel during the litigation, and no information whatever regarding the number of hours worked, the hourly rates sought for that work, the reasonableness of those rates, or the overall lodestar, the Court cannot determine whether the fee award sought is reasonable. Accordingly, the motion is denied without prejudice to a further submission. Plaintiff should be prepared at the hearing to propose a date by which that filing will be made and a continued hearing date on the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINA CONTRERAS VS. MAXIM CRANE WORKS, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer Venue To Sacramento County Superior Court
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, April 29, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT MAXIM CRANE WORKS, L.P.'s Motion To Transfer Venue To Sacramento County Superior Court. Defendant Maxim Crane Works, L.P.'s ("L.P.") motion to change venue to Sacramento is denied. Plaintiff's choice of venue is presumptively correct and L.P. has the burden of showing that plaintiff commenced the case in the wrong court. (See Easton v. Superior Court (1970) 12 Cal.App.3d 243, 247.) Even though L.P. registered with California, its registration does not list a principal place of business in California. (Busser Decl., Ex. C.) Therefore, L.P. can be sued in any county of the state. (Code Civ. Proc. § 395.5; Easton, 12 Cal.App.3d at 246-247.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
